content:
In xarray's iter implementation:

fn iter(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = NonNull<T::PointedTo>> + '_ {
    let mut index = 0;

    // SAFETY: self.xa is always valid by the type invariant.
    iter::once(unsafe {
        bindings::xa_find(self.xa.get(), &mut index, usize::MAX, bindings::XA_PRESENT)
    })
    .chain(iter::from_fn(move || {
        // SAFETY: self.xa is always valid by the type invariant.
        Some(unsafe {
            bindings::xa_find_after(self.xa.get(), &mut index, usize::MAX, bindings::XA_PRESENT)
        })
    }))
    .map_while(|ptr| NonNull::new(ptr.cast()))
}

The current return type uses + '_, which promises that the RPIT (return-position impl Trait) opaque type outlives the lifetime of &self. However, I believe what we actually want here is the opposite: the lifetime of &self outlives the lifetime of the opaque type.

This would be correctly expressed as:

fn iter(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = NonNull<T::PointedTo>> + use<'_, T>

Unfortunately, use bounds are not available in Rust 1.78.0, which is the current MSRV for Rust-for-Linux. IIUC, we might want to at least leave a TODO comment to indicate this mismatch and document the intended future improvement.

Any thoughts?

Reference: https://rust-lang.github.io/rfcs/3498-lifetime-capture-rules-2024.html#the-outlives-trick

to quote from your link:

It works anyway in this specific case only because the lifetime of the returned opaque type is exactly equal to the lifetime 'a. Because equality is symmetric, the fact that our promise is the wrong way around doesn’t matter.

I think this also applies here, the returned iterator lives exactly for '_.

Now if we could use use<>, then it might make sense to change it, but I don't think that we need a comment, since there are a lot of other places that could make use of this change as well, so I don't think we should single this one out
Thanks, that makes sense. I was mostly thinking about it from an accuracy perspective, but agreed that it's not critical to call it out in this context.

It doesn't sound like a bad idea to list the ones we know we could eventually change in an issue or similar if someone wants to go through them. It can also serve as a guide for others looking into use<> later on.
If it eventually turns out to be useful, I'm happy to send a patch.

I think having it in an issue would be definitely useful.

As for a patch, i.e. as a comment, that sounds OK to me too, but @Benno Lossin mentioned there would be many cases. I only see a few -> impl ... + '... instances at the moment from a quick grep, though.
(It is true that, if we keep adding more, it may not be great to have a comment for each.)

Miguel Ojeda said:

I think having it in an issue would be definitely useful.

As for a patch, i.e. as a comment, that sounds OK to me too, but Benno Lossin mentioned there would be many cases. I only see a few -> impl ... + '... instances at the moment from a quick grep, though.

I didn't actually check how many instances we had :sweat_smile: but since it comes up a lot when working with borrowing iterators, I thought we might have several...

Miguel Ojeda said:

(It is true that, if we keep adding more, it may not be great to have a comment for each.)

yeah, I think having an issue is a good idea
Then I'll file an issue with the -> impl ... + '... instances I can find for now. Thanks for the discussion!
